title: Magnetization alignment in spin-transfer-torque magnetic random-access memory
authors: Afan Terko, George Lertzman-Lepofsky, Dieter Suess, Claas Abert, Erol Girt
abstract: Reliable operation of perpendicular spin-transfer-torque magnetic random-access memory (p-STT-MRAM) requires control of magnetic alignment within the synthetic antiferromagnet (SAF) reference layer. At nanopillar dimensions, however, devices can exhibit magnetic states that are absent in extended thin films. We present a systematic micromagnetic study of 30 nm-diameter three-layer p-STT-MRAM nanopillars using experimentally motivated material parameters, and map equilibrium states as functions of bilinear and biquadratic interlayer exchange coupling. Phase diagrams show that introducing asymmetry between the SAF layers in saturation magnetization, anisotropy, and thickness reduces the coupling strength required to stabilize antiparallel SAF states and suppress competing configurations. Minimum-energy path calculations show that, for noncollinear antiparallel SAF states, increasing SAF asymmetry can raise SAF reversal barriers while lowering the free-layer barrier; this trade-off is absent for collinear antiparallel SAF states. Stray fields also significantly modify both SAF and free-layer energy barriers. To support the design of p-STT-MRAM devices with either collinear or noncollinear antiparallel SAF reference states, we publicly release the simulation dataset covering 4374 distinct device configurations.
summary: This study uses micromagnetic simulations to show how structural asymmetry in MRAM nanopillars can stabilize desired magnetic states. By tuning magnetization and anisotropy, researchers can suppress competing configurations and control energy barriers. The authors have released a large dataset of 4374 device configurations to aid in MRAM design.
main_problem: Controlling magnetic alignment within the synthetic antiferromagnet (SAF) reference layer of perpendicular spin-transfer-torque MRAM (p-STT-MRAM) nanopillars to prevent unwanted magnetic states.
main_result: Introducing asymmetry in saturation magnetization, anisotropy, and thickness between SAF layers reduces the coupling strength needed to stabilize antiparallel states and can raise SAF reversal barriers.
method: Systematic micromagnetic simulations using experimentally motivated material parameters and minimum-energy path calculations.
model_or_system: 30 nm-diameter three-layer p-STOG-MRAM nanopillars consisting of a free layer and a synthetic antiferromagnet (SAF) reference layer.
key_observables: Equilibrium magnetic states, phase diagrams of coupling, and magnetic reversal energy barriers.
important_parameters: Bilinear and biquadratic interlayer exchange coupling, saturation magnetization, magnetic anisotropy, layer thickness, and stray fields.
main_assumptions: The study uses micromagnetic modeling with parameters motivated by experimental data.
figures_summary: The paper includes phase diagrams mapping equilibrium states and calculations of minimum-energy paths for magnetic reversal.
paper_structure: The paper presents a micromagnetic study of nanopillar equilibrium states, followed by an analysis of phase diagrams under varying coupling, an investigation of energy barriers via minimum-energy paths, and the release of a large simulation dataset.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
